## Falsifiability

- **Primary endpoint:** PAM-13 Δ ≥ 5.4 points (MCID)
- **Significance level:** α = 0.05 (two-sided) for primary analysis; Bonferroni correction applied only to secondary endpoints (≤2 comparisons) with α = 0.025 for each secondary test. **Harmonisation note:** This threshold supersedes any conflicting values in THEORY.md; the single adopted value is α=0.05 (two-sided) for primary, with Bonferroni-adjusted α=0.025 for secondary endpoints.
- **Power:** 0.80
- **Sample size:** N ≥ 55 per group (see Sample size calculation)
- **Stopping rule:** Adverse event rate >5% in intervention arm (any AE, assessed monthly by DSMB)
- **Interim analysis:** Planned at N=100 (50 per group) for futility only (O'Brien-Fleming boundary, α-spending = 0.005). If conditional power <20%, trial will be stopped for futility.
- **Primary analysis:** Intention-to-treat (ITT); per-protocol sensitivity analysis as secondary

## Falsifiability

- **Primary endpoint:** PAM-13 Δ ≥ 5.4 points (MCID)
- **Significance level:** α = 0.05 (two-sided) for primary analysis; Bonferroni correction applied only to secondary endpoints (≤2 comparisons) with α = 0.025 for each secondary test. **Harmonisation note:** This threshold supersedes any conflicting values in THEORY.md; the single adopted value is α=0.05 (two-sided) for primary, with Bonferroni-adjusted α=0.025 for secondary endpoints. No other α thresholds (e.g., p < 0.001) are used.

## Falsifiability

- **Primary endpoint:** PAM-13 Δ ≥ 5.4 points (MCID)
- **Significance level:** α = 0.05 (two-sided) for primary analysis; Bonferroni correction applied only to secondary endpoints (≤2 comparisons) with α = 0.025 for each secondary test. **Harmonisation note:** This threshold supersedes any conflicting values in THEORY.md; the single adopted value is α=0.05 (two-sided) for primary, with Bonferroni-adjusted α=0.025 for secondary endpoints. No other α thresholds (e.g., p < 0.001) are used.
- **Power:** 0.80
- **Sample size:** N ≥ 55 per group (see Sample size calculation)
- **Stopping rule:** Adverse event rate >5% in intervention arm (any AE, assessed monthly by DSMB)
- **Interim analysis:** Planned at N=100 (50 per group) for futility only (O'Brien-Fleming boundary, α-spending = 0.005). If conditional power <20%, trial will be stopped for futility.
- **Primary analysis:** Intention-to-treat (ITT); per-protocol sensitivity analysis as secondary

## Risk matrix

| # | Risk | Probability | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|------|-------------|--------|------------|
| 1 | Low recruitment (N < 132 in 12 months) | Medium | High | Multi-site recruitment (TSU, University of Copenhagen); monthly monitoring; extend enrollment period by 6 months if needed |
| 2 | LLM hallucination causing patient harm | Low | Critical | Medical oversight by Co-I (MD); real-time LLM output filtering; mandatory disclaimer; adverse event reporting |
| 3 | Data privacy breach (GDPR non-compliance) | Low | Critical | Pseudonymisation; encrypted storage; annual GDPR audit; data processing agreement with all partners |
| 4 | PAM-13 license withdrawal by Insignia Health | Low | High | Backup outcome measure (Patient Activation Measure short form); early license negotiation |
| 5 | Reference integrity failure (e.g., incorrect PMID) | Medium | Medium | Pre-submission reference audit by two independent reviewers; use DOI where available; automated PMID validation |
| 6 | Dropout rate >20% | Medium | Medium | Retention strategies (reminders, incentives); per-protocol sensitivity analysis; oversample by 20% |
| 7 | σ estimate off by >20% from pilot | Medium | Medium | Pilot phase (N=20 per group); recalculate sample size; adjust enrollment target if needed

## Limitations

1. **Single-centre design:** Results may not generalise to other healthcare settings or populations.
2. **Short follow-up (6 months):** Long-term effects on patient activation and clinical outcomes are not assessed.
3. **Self-reported outcomes:** PAM-13 and MMAS-8 are subject to social desirability and recall bias.
4. **Digital literacy confound:** Participants with low digital literacy may have differential engagement with the LLM intervention; not controlled for in current design.
5. **Hawthorne effect:** Both arms receive attention from study staff; control arm may show higher-than-usual activation.
6. **Placebo effect of LLM interaction:** Novelty of AI interaction may inflate effect size in intervention arm.
7. **Sample size based on assumed σ = 10:** If true SD is larger, study may be underpowered; sensitivity analysis planned.
8. **Reference integrity:** One reference (Hibbard 2004) had incorrect PMID in earlier version; corrected to PMID 15333167. All references will be re-audited before submission.

## Limitations

1. **Single-centre design:** Results may not generalise to other healthcare settings or populations.
2. **Short follow-up (6 months):** Long-term effects on patient activation and clinical outcomes are not assessed.
3. **Self-reported outcomes:** PAM-13 and MMAS-8 are subject to social desirability and recall bias.
4. **Digital literacy confound:** Participants with low digital literacy may have differential engagement with the LLM intervention; not controlled for in current design.
5. **Hawthorne effect:** Both arms receive attention from study staff; control arm may show higher-than-usual activation.
6. **Placebo effect of LLM interaction:** Novelty of AI interaction may inflate effect size in intervention arm.
7. **Sample size based on assumed σ = 10:** If true SD is larger, study may be underpowered; sensitivity analysis planned.
8. **Reference integrity:** One reference (Hibbard 2004) had incorrect PMID in earlier version; corrected to PMID 15333167. All references will be re-audited before submission.
